mitre_attack: T1590.001 - Gather Victim Network Information: Domain Properties
cwe: CWE-444 - Inconsistent Interpretation of HTTP Requests (HTTP Request/Response Smuggling)
CWE-639 - Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
CWE-525 - Use of Web Browser Cache Containing Sensitive Information

threat.title: CDN Cache Poisoning and Web Cache Deception Attacks
threat.description: Content Delivery Network (CDN) cache poisoning exploits inconsistencies between CDN cache key generation and origin server request handling to inject malicious content into edge caches, affecting all users receiving the cached response. Web cache deception tricks CDNs into caching sensitive user-specific pages by exploiting path confusion vulnerabilities. According to research by Omer Gil and James Kettle, web cache poisoning affects major CDN providers including Cloudflare, Fastly, and Akamai. In 2018, researchers demonstrated practical cache poisoning attacks against PayPal, leading to widespread XSS vulnerabilities. These attacks enable threat actors to serve malicious JavaScript, steal credentials, deface websites, and exfiltrate sensitive data from cached authenticated responses. The 2024 Cloudflare incident affecting multiple customers demonstrated the persistent risk of cache poisoning in production CDN infrastructure.

versions_affected: All CDN providers without proper cache key normalization

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker discovers CDN endpoint serving dynamic application through proxy (e.g., cdn.example.com)
2. Performs reconnaissance to identify cache key composition (typically URL path + query string)
3. Discovers unkeyed headers (X-Forwarded-Host, X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL) not included in cache key
4. Crafts malicious request with poisoned unkeyed header:
   GET /static/app.js HTTP/1.1
   Host: cdn.example.com
   X-Forwarded-Host: evil.com
5. Origin server uses X-Forwarded-Host to construct resource URLs in response
6. CDN caches poisoned response with evil.com references using only path as cache key
7. Subsequent users requesting /static/app.js receive cached poisoned response
8. Malicious JavaScript executes in victim browsers, stealing credentials and session tokens
9. Alternatively, attacker exploits path confusion:
   GET /api/user/profile/nonexistent.css HTTP/1.1
10. CDN caches authenticated /api/user/profile response thinking it's static CSS
11. Attacker retrieves cached sensitive profile data without authentication
12. Persists until cache TTL expires (often 24+ hours)
prerequisites: CDN deployed in front of application origin server
Origin server uses unkeyed headers in response generation
CDN cache key does not include all request components affecting response
No cache-control headers preventing inappropriate caching
Path normalization differs between CDN and origin server

cve_references.description: Atlassian Jira cache poisoning vulnerability allowing attackers to inject malicious content via unkeyed X-Forwarded-Host header, affecting Jira behind reverse proxies and CDNs
nghttp2 HTTP/2 implementation allows cache poisoning attacks via header injection in CONTINUATION frames, affecting CDN configurations using nghttp2
VMware vCenter Server cache poisoning vulnerability via X-Forwarded-For header manipulation, enabling SSRF and cache poisoning when deployed behind CDN
Node.js HTTP parser allows header injection via X-Forwarded-Host enabling cache poisoning attacks against Node.js applications behind CDNs
Undici (Node.js HTTP client) content-length handling vulnerability enabling request smuggling and cache poisoning attacks

real_world_breaches.incident: PayPal Web Cache Deception via Path Confusion
real_world_breaches.impact: Researchers demonstrated cache deception attack allowing theft of PayPal user account details, transaction history, and credit card information through CDN cache exploitation. Affected all PayPal users until remediation. Vulnerability allowed attackers to trick victims into loading URLs like paypal.com/myaccount/settings/nonexistent.css causing CDN to cache authenticated pages as static resources.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Path confusion between PayPal origin servers and Akamai CDN. PayPal ignored trailing path segments while Akamai used full path including .css extension to determine cacheability

real_world_breaches.impact: Cloudflare cache poisoning vulnerability affected multiple customer websites for approximately 6 hours. Malicious content was cached and served to end users through edge locations. Incident triggered by improper handling of Vary headers combined with HTTP/2 header normalization differences. Estimated 200,000+ sites affected globally.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Inconsistent header normalization between HTTP/2 and HTTP/1.1 protocols at Cloudflare edge, combined with insufficient cache key validation for Vary header processing

real_world_breaches.incident: Fastly CDN Cache Poisoning Research Campaign
real_world_breaches.impact: Security researchers at PortSwigger demonstrated practical cache poisoning attacks against Fastly CDN affecting multiple high-profile customers. Attacks exploited unkeyed X-Forwarded-Scheme and X-Forwarded-Proto headers to inject malicious content. Proof-of-concept attacks successfully poisoned caches for authentication pages, enabling credential harvesting.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Fastly cache keys omitted security-relevant headers (X-Forwarded-Scheme, X-Forwarded-Proto, X-Forwarded-Host) while origin servers used these headers in response generation

real_world_breaches.incident: Akamai Edge Server Cache Confusion Attack
real_world_breaches.impact: Researchers demonstrated cache poisoning attacks against Akamai's edge servers using HTTP/2 request splitting. Attacks enabled XSS injection into cached responses affecting downstream users for cache TTL duration (typically 24 hours). Affected Fortune 500 companies using Akamai CDN infrastructure.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: HTTP/2 header parsing inconsistencies between Akamai edge servers and origin servers, combined with insufficient validation of pseudo-headers

real_world_breaches.impact: Web cache deception vulnerability in GitHub Pages allowed attackers to cache authenticated user sessions and private repository information. Vulnerability exploited path confusion where GitHub served authenticated content for paths like /user/repos/fake.css while CDN cached based on .css extension.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: GitHub ignored trailing path segments in routing while Fastly CDN used full path including extensions to determine cache policy

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Web cache poisoning in Shopify CDN via X-Forwarded-Host header allowing XSS injection into cached checkout pages
Cache deception in major SaaS platform allowing theft of customer API keys through CDN path confusion
Cloudflare cache poisoning enabling persistent XSS across multiple customer zones via Vary header manipulation

security_research.title: Practical Web Cache Poisoning: Escalating to XSS and Beyond
security_research.key_finding: Demonstrated practical cache poisoning attacks against major CDN providers (Cloudflare, Fastly, Akamai) using unkeyed headers. Discovered that most CDNs fail to include security-relevant headers like X-Forwarded-Host in cache keys, enabling widespread poisoning attacks. Research identified vulnerable patterns across 33% of tested sites.

security_research.title: Web Cache Deception Attack
security_research.key_finding: Introduced web cache deception attack exploiting path confusion between origin servers and CDNs. Demonstrated practical attacks against PayPal, GitHub, and multiple banking institutions. Attack enables caching of authenticated user-specific content by appending fake static file extensions to dynamic URLs.

security_research.title: HTTP Desync Attacks: Smashing into the Cell Next Door
security_research.key_finding: Demonstrated HTTP request smuggling attacks enabling cache poisoning across major CDN providers. Showed how HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/2 translation inconsistencies create opportunities for request smuggling leading to cache poisoning. Affected Akamai, Cloudflare, AWS CloudFront.

security_research.title: Cached and Confused: Web Cache Deception in the Wild
security_research.key_finding: Large-scale study identifying web cache deception vulnerabilities across 340 popular websites. Found that 7% of Alexa top 5,000 sites were vulnerable. Research quantified real-world prevalence and demonstrated automated detection techniques for cache deception vulnerabilities.

security_research.title: CDN Cannon: Exploiting CDN Back-to-Origin Strategies for Amplification Attacks
security_research.key_finding: Demonstrated amplification attacks exploiting CDN back-to-origin request patterns combined with cache poisoning to create persistent DDoS conditions. Research showed how poisoned cache entries can trigger recursive amplification affecting both CDN infrastructure and origin servers.

mitigations.title: Implement Comprehensive Cache Key Normalization with Vary Headers
mitigations.description: Configure CDN cache keys to include all request components that affect response content, including security-relevant headers. Use Vary headers to explicitly declare which request headers influence the response. Implement strict cache key normalization to prevent attackers from exploiting differences in how CDN and origin interpret requests.
iso27001_controls: A.14.1.2 - Securing application services on public networks
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Deploy edge worker or update CDN configuration with cache key normalization
Test that dangerous headers (X-Forwarded-Host, X-Original-URL) are blocked or sanitized
Verify cache key includes all security-relevant request components
Test that authenticated requests are not cached
Attempt cache poisoning attack with X-Forwarded-Host header and verify it fails
Monitor cache HIT ratio to ensure normalization doesn't degrade performance
Validate Vary headers are present in cached responses
Review cache logs for blocked poisoning attempts

mitigations.title: Implement Path Normalization and Web Cache Deception Prevention
mitigations.description: Standardize path handling between CDN and origin servers to prevent path confusion attacks. Implement strict path validation, normalize paths before caching decisions, and ensure CDN cache policy matches origin routing logic. Prevent caching of dynamic content with static file extensions.
iso27001_controls: A.14.1.2 - Securing application services on public networks
nist_csf: PR.PT-1 - Audit/log records are determined and documented
validation_steps: Deploy Flask application with path normalization middleware
Test legitimate static resources are cached correctly (/static/app.js)
Test dynamic endpoints with fake extensions are blocked (/api/profile/fake.css)
Verify authenticated endpoints never cache (check Cache-Control headers)
Attempt path traversal with ../ sequences and verify they're normalized
Test path confusion with URL encoding (%2e%2e%2f) and verify blocking
Monitor logs for cache deception attempts
Validate all dynamic routes return Cache-Control: no-store

mitigations.title: Deploy Cache-Control Policies with Origin-Side Cache Headers
mitigations.description: Implement comprehensive Cache-Control headers at origin servers to explicitly declare caching policies. Use cache directives (no-store, private, public) based on content sensitivity. Implement short TTLs for dynamic content and use Cache-Control: no-transform to prevent CDN manipulation.
validation_steps: Deploy Express.js application with cache control middleware
Verify static assets return Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable
Verify API endpoints return Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate
Test authenticated endpoints return Cache-Control: private
Verify CDN-Cache-Control headers are present (Cloudflare/Fastly specific)
Test request with X-Forwarded-Host header is blocked with 403
Check error responses (4xx, 5xx) are never cached
Monitor CDN logs to confirm cache policies are respected

mitigations.title: Implement CDN Security Configurations and Monitoring
mitigations.description: Configure CDN-specific security features including request validation, custom cache rules, WAF policies, and real-time monitoring. Use CDN provider security features (Cloudflare WAF, Fastly VCL security rules, Akamai Kona Site Defender) to detect and block cache poisoning attempts. Implement logging and alerting for suspicious cache behavior.
iso27001_controls: A.16.1.4 - Assessment of and decision on information security events
nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data are collected and correlated
DE.CM-1 - Network is monitored to detect cybersecurity events
RS.AN-1 - Notifications from detection systems are investigated
validation_steps: Deploy Terraform configuration to Cloudflare
Verify WAF rules are active in Cloudflare dashboard
Test cache rules: authenticated requests should bypass cache
Attempt request with X-Forwarded-Host header and verify 403 response
Verify static assets (/static/*) are cached with long TTL
Check Cloudflare Analytics for blocked requests
Configure log shipping to SIEM (Splunk, Elastic, Azure Sentinel)
Set up alerts for cache poisoning attempts (>10 blocked requests/minute)
Review Cloudflare Security Events dashboard weekly
Test rate limiting with load testing tool

indicators: Unusual spike in cache MISS rate for static resources
Requests containing X-Forwarded-Host, X-Original-URL, or X-Rewrite-URL headers
Cache HIT for URLs that should never be cached (/api/*, /admin/*)
Multiple requests from single IP with varying unkeyed headers
Dynamic endpoints receiving requests with static file extensions
Repeated cache purge requests from suspicious sources
Anomalous Vary header values in cached responses
Cache TTL values significantly longer than configured
Origin server receiving Host headers different from expected domain
Suspicious patterns in CDN logs: path traversal attempts, encoded characters in URLs

log_queries.query:
// Detect CDN cache poisoning attempts
Cloudflare_CL
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where EdgeResponseStatus == 403 or EdgeResponseStatus == 400
| extend DangerousHeader = case(
    ClientRequestHeaders contains "x-forwarded-host", "X-Forwarded-Host",
    ClientRequestHeaders contains "x-original-url", "X-Original-URL",
    ClientRequestHeaders contains "x-rewrite-url", "X-Rewrite-URL",
    "None"
  )
| where DangerousHeader != "None"
| summarize
    AttemptCount = count(),
    UniqueURLs = dcount(ClientRequestURI),
    FirstSeen = min(TimeGenerated),
    LastSeen = max(TimeGenerated),
    SampleHeaders = take_any(ClientRequestHeaders)
    by ClientIP, DangerousHeader
| where AttemptCount > 5
| order by AttemptCount desc

index=cdn sourcetype=cloudflare_logs
| eval is_cache_poison=if(
    (like(client_request_headers, "%x-forwarded-host%") OR
     like(client_request_headers, "%x-original-url%") OR
     like(client_request_uri, "%/api/%") AND (like(client_request_uri, "%.css") OR like(client_request_uri, "%.js"))),
    1, 0)
| where is_cache_poison=1
| stats
    count as attempts,
    dc(client_request_uri) as unique_urls,
    values(edge_response_status) as response_codes,
    earliest(_time) as first_attempt,
    latest(_time) as last_attempt
    by client_ip
| where attempts > 10
| eval duration=tostring(last_attempt-first_attempt, "duration")
| sort -attempts

cdn.provider: ("cloudflare" OR "fastly" OR "akamai") AND (
  (http.request.headers.x-forwarded-host: * OR
   http.request.headers.x-original-url: * OR
   http.request.headers.x-rewrite-url: *) OR
  (url.path: (/api/* OR /admin/* OR /account/*) AND
   url.extension: (css OR js OR jpg OR png))
) AND
http.response.status_code: (403 OR 400 OR 404)

service:cdn-edge
(@cache_status:MISS @url.path:(/api/* OR /admin/*) @http.status_code:200) OR
(@http.request.headers.x-forwarded-host:* OR @http.request.headers.x-original-url:*)
status:(error OR warning)
| group by @network.client.ip
| count > 10

tools.name: Cloudflare Analytics
tools.usage: Monitor cache HIT/MISS ratios, security events, and WAF blocks

tools.name: Fastly Real-Time Log Streaming
tools.usage: Stream CDN logs to SIEM for cache poisoning detection

tools.name: Akamai SIEM Integration
tools.usage: Forward security events to enterprise SIEM platforms

tools.name: Burp Suite
tools.usage: Test for cache poisoning vulnerabilities using Param Miner extension

tools.name: Web Cache Vulnerability Scanner
tools.usage: Automated detection of cache poisoning and deception vulnerabilities
